meta.title: Nexus Talent
meta.description: Case study of Nexus Talent: AI-driven job description analysis with prompt engineering, pnpm monorepo, Zod shared contracts, Screaming Architecture and JWT httpOnly auth.

header.title: Nexus Talent
header.short: SaaS platform for job application analysis: structured AI with Groq (Llama 3.3 70B), 200-line prompt engineering, pnpm monorepo with 4 packages, and Screaming Architecture.

summary.heading: Summary
summary.text: Nexus Talent is a SaaS platform for intelligent job application analysis. Users paste a job description, the backend sends it to Groq (Llama 3.3 70B) with a ~200-line engineered system prompt, and the response is validated with Zod before hitting the UI. The output: a weighted skill matrix, ATS keywords, gap analysis, outreach messages, and actionable tips. All built on a pnpm monorepo with 4 packages (web, server, shared, e2e), shared Zod schemas between frontend and backend, 341 frontend tests, 45 backend tests, JWT in httpOnly cookies (XSS-immune), and an 18-component design system with Framer Motion.

problem.heading: The problem
problem.text: Developers spend hours manually dissecting each job description : figuring out which skills to highlight, which ATS keywords to include, and how to tailor their profile for every vacancy. The result is generic applications that neither pass automated filters nor stand out to recruiters.

solution.heading: The Solution
list: Server-side AI pipeline with Groq (Llama 3.3 70B) and 200-line prompt engineering that extracts skills, ATS keywords, and gaps in structured JSON.
Zod validation at both the LLM input and output boundaries ensures the UI receives typed, predictable data : no hallucinated or malformed responses.
JWT authentication in httpOnly cookies (XSS-immune), bcrypt hashing, and a complete login, registration, and Google OAuth flow.
Screaming Architecture across a 4-package monorepo (web, server, shared, e2e) with shared Zod schemas that keep frontend and backend types in sync by design.
Editorial design system with 18 component families, Framer Motion spring physics, dark mode, and WCAG AA contrast.

architecture.heading: Architecture & decisions
architecture.text: Nexus Talent follows Screaming Architecture on a pnpm monorepo with 4 packages. Features (analysis, auth, history, landing, settings) are isolated domains with their own hooks, components, schemas, tests, and API layer. The shared package contains the Zod schemas that are the single source of truth for both frontend and backend : if a schema changes, TypeScript breaks on both sides. Desync is impossible by design.
list: Every feature is self-contained: its own hooks, components, schemas, and API layer. No generic utils/helpers folders.
Zod schemas in the shared package are the single source of truth. Frontend and backend share the exact same types.
JWT in httpOnly cookies : the frontend never sees the token. XSS-immune by design.
The Groq API key is never exposed to the client. The frontend sends only the JD; the backend constructs the prompt, calls Groq, validates with Zod, and returns clean data.
Spec-Driven Development: every change went through 6 planning phases (explore → propose → spec → design → tasks → apply → verify), catching ~15 edge cases before writing code.

implementation.heading: Featured implementation
implementation.hookDescription: The AI flow is guarded by Zod validation at both input and output boundaries. Input passes through jobDescriptionSchema before reaching the LLM, and the response is validated with skillMatrixSchema.safeParse(). If the AI hallucinates or returns malformed JSON, the error is captured as a manageable application state instead of crashing the UI.

accessibility.heading: Accessibility
accessibility.lead: The entire design system respects prefers-reduced-motion on every Framer Motion animated component. WCAG AA contrast is maintained in both light and dark modes.
list: useReducedMotion on every animated component to respect system preferences.
WCAG AA contrast verified across the warm off-white + espresso + terracotta palette in both modes.
Keyboard navigation preserved across the analysis flow, forms, and modals.
Async states with semantic loading, success, and error feedback through the toast system.
Predictable focus order during multi-step interactions (analysis, outreach editing).

performance.heading: Performance & results
list: Main bundle reduced from 596 kB to 414 kB (-30%) with lazy loading and chunk splitting.
CSS bundle at 79 kB (23 kB gzip).
framer-motion and phosphor-icons in separate chunks for on-demand loading.
1-year cache headers for static assets. font-display: swap on all typefaces.
SVG favicon at ~1 kB replacing a 668 kB icon.

challenges.heading: Challenges & learnings
list: Prompt engineering required to get Llama 3.3 70B to return consistent JSON with 6 nested sections. 200 lines of iterated instructions to achieve stable output.
Tailwind v4 with custom @theme: text-white breaks if you don't declare --color-white, @utility can't nest inside @layer. Breaking changes discovered at runtime.
Green tests don't mean production-ready: CSS layers, React portals + event bubbling, mocks vs real API : 4 bugs passed tests but failed in production.
The Tailwind plugin in Vitest caused 91s of collect time in CI for 86 test files. Removing it cut the time to ~2s.

improvements.heading: What could be improved
list: Add personalized CV generation from the extracted skill matrix, with multiple templates.
Benchmark system: compare a JD analysis against the user's profile and suggest courses or projects to close gaps.
Implement web scraping to automatically fetch the JD from a vacancy URL.
